{{Short description|Alleged impact on behavior}}
'''Priming''' is a concept in [[psychology]] and [[psycholinguistics]] to describe how exposure to one [[stimulus (psychology)|stimulus]] may influence a response to a subsequent stimulus, without conscious guidance or intention.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Weingarten E, Chen Q, McAdams M, Yi J, Hepler J, Albarracín D | title = From primed concepts to action: A meta-analysis of the behavioral effects of incidentally presented words | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 142 | issue = 5 | pages = 472–97 | date = May 2016 | pmid = 26689090 | pmc = 5783538 | doi = 10.1037/bul0000030 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | chapter = Studying the Mind in the Middle: A Practical Guide to Priming and Automaticity Research | title = Handbook of Research Methods in Social Psychology| vauthors = Bargh JA, Chartrand TL |publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2000|veditors=Reis H, Judd C |location=New York, NY|pages=1–39}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Shapir OM, Shapir-Tidhar MH, Shtudiner Z| title = Priming effect across framing, culture, and gender: Evidence from the academia | journal = Managerial and Decision Economics | volume = 44| issue = 7 | pages = 3758–3768 | date = October 2023 | ssrn = 4387810| doi = 10.1002/mde.3918 }}</ref> The '''priming effect''' is the positive or negative effect of a rapidly presented stimulus (priming stimulus) on the processing of a second stimulus (target stimulus) that appears shortly after. Generally speaking, the generation of priming effect depends on the existence of some positive or negative relationship between priming and target stimuli. For example, the word ''nurse'' might be recognized more quickly following the word ''doctor'' than following the word ''bread''. Priming can be [[perceptual]], associative, repetitive, positive, negative, affective, [[semantic]], or [[wikt:conceptual|conceptual]]. Priming effects involve word recognition, semantic processing, attention, unconscious processing, and many other issues, and are related to differences in various writing systems.  How quickly this effect occurs is contested;<ref name="tulv">{{cite journal| vauthors = Tulving E, Schacter DL, Stark HA |year=1982|title=Priming Effects in Word Fragment Completion are independent of Recognition Memory|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition|volume=8|issue=4|pages=336–342|doi=10.1037/0278-7393.8.4.336|s2cid=143013790 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bargh JA, Gollwitzer PM, Lee-Chai A, Barndollar K, Trötschel R | title = The automated will: nonconscious activation and pursuit of behavioral goals | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 81 | issue = 6 | pages = 1014–27 | date = December 2001 | pmid = 11761304 | pmc = 3005626 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.81.6.1014 }}</ref> some researchers claim that priming effects are almost instantaneous.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ben-Haim MS, Chajut E, Hassin RR, Algom D | title = Speeded naming or naming speed? The automatic effect of object speed on performance | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology: General | volume = 144 | issue = 2 | pages = 326–38 | date = April 2015 | pmid = 25559652 | doi = 10.1037/a0038569 | url = https://zenodo.org/record/931452 }}</ref>

Priming works most effectively when the two stimuli are in the same [[modality (semiotics)|modality]]. For example, visual priming works best with visual cues and verbal priming works best with verbal cues. But priming also occurs between modalities,<ref>Several researchers, for example, have used cross-modal priming to investigate [[Syntax|syntactic]] deficits in individuals with damage to [[Broca's area]] of the brain.  See the following:

* {{cite journal | vauthors = Zurif E, Swinney D, Prather P, Solomon J, Bushell C | title = An on-line analysis of syntactic processing in Broca's and Wernicke's aphasia | journal = Brain and Language | volume = 45 | issue = 3 | pages = 448–64 | date = October 1993 | pmid = 8269334 | doi = 10.1006/brln.1993.1054 | s2cid = 8791285 | doi-access = free }}
* {{cite journal | vauthors = Swinney D, Zurif E, Prather P, Love T | title = Neurological distribution of processing resources underlying language comprehension. | journal = Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience | date = March 1996 | volume = 8 | issue = 2 | pages = 174–84 | doi = 10.1162/jocn.1996.8.2.174 | pmid = 23971422 | s2cid = 1836757 }}
*For an overview, see also {{cite book | vauthors = Zurif EB | date = 1995 | chapter = Brain Regions of Relevance to Syntactic Processing | title = Knowledge of Meaning: An Introduction to Semantic Theory | veditors = Larson R, Segal G | publisher = MIT Press }}</ref> or between [[Semantics|semantically]] related words such as "doctor" and "nurse".<ref name="MS71">{{cite journal| vauthors = Meyer DE, Schvaneveldt RW |year=1971|title=Facilitation in recognizing pairs of words: Evidence of a dependence between retrieval operations|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology|volume=90|issue=2|pages=227–234|doi=10.1037/h0031564|pmid=5134329|s2cid=36672941}}</ref><ref>
{{cite journal
 | vauthors = Friederici AD, Steinhauer K, Frisch S
 | title = Lexical integration: sequential effects of syntactic and semantic information
 | journal = Memory & Cognition | volume = 27 | issue = 3 | pages = 438–53
 | date = May 1999 | pmid = 10355234 | doi = 10.3758/BF03211539
 | quote = ''Semantic priming'' refers to the finding that word recognition is typically faster when the target word (e.g., ''doctor'') is preceded by a semantically related prime word (e.g., ''nurse''). 
| doi-access = free}}
</ref>

In 2012, a great amount of priming research was thrown into doubt as part of the [[replication crisis]].  Many of the landmark studies that found effects of priming were unable to be replicated in new trials using the same mechanisms.<ref name="Bower2012" />  The [[Experimenter Effect|experimenter effect]] may have allowed the people running the experiments to subtly influence them to reach the desired result, and [[publication bias]] tended to mean that shocking and positive results were seen as interesting and more likely to be published than studies that failed to show any effect of priming.  The result is that the efficacy of priming may have been greatly overstated in earlier literature, or have been entirely illusory.<ref name=":3" /><ref name="Doyen2012" />

==Types==
===Positive and negative priming===
The terms ''positive'' and ''negative'' priming refer to when priming affects the speed of processing. A positive prime speeds up processing, while a negative prime lowers the speed to slower than un-primed levels.<ref name=neg>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mayr S, Buchner A| doi=10.1027/0044-3409.215.1.35 |title= Negative Priming as a Memory Phenomenon| year=2007 | journal= Zeitschrift für Psychologie | pages=35–51 | volume=215 | issue=1}}</ref> Positive priming is caused by simply experiencing the stimulus,<ref name=reis>Reisberg, Daniel: ''Cognition: Exploring the Science of the Mind'' (2007), page 255, 517.</ref> while negative priming is caused by experiencing the stimulus, and then ignoring it.<ref name=neg/><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Neumann E, DeSchepper BG | title = Costs and benefits of target activation and distractor inhibition in selective attention | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition | volume = 17 | issue = 6 | pages = 1136–45 | date = November 1991 | pmid = 1838386 | doi = 10.1037/0278-7393.17.6.1136 }}</ref> Positive priming effects happen even if the prime is not consciously perceived.<ref name=reis/> The effects of positive and negative priming are visible in [[event-related potential]] (ERP) readings.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bentin S, McCarthy G, Wood CC | title = Event-related potentials, lexical decision and semantic priming | journal = Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology | volume = 60 | issue = 4 | pages = 343–55 | date = April 1985 | pmid = 2579801 | doi = 10.1016/0013-4694(85)90008-2 }}</ref>

Positive priming is thought to be caused by spreading activation.<ref name=reis/> This means that the first stimulus activates parts of a particular representation or [[association (psychology)|association]] in [[memory]] just before carrying out an action or task. The representation is already partially activated when the second stimulus is encountered, so less additional activation is needed for one to become consciously aware of it.

Negative priming is more difficult to explain. Many models have been hypothesized, but currently the most widely accepted are the distractor inhibition and episodic retrieval models.<ref name=neg/> In the distractor inhibition model, the activation of ignored stimuli is inhibited by the brain.<ref name=neg/> The episodic retrieval model hypothesizes that ignored items are flagged 'do-not-respond' by the brain. Later, when the brain acts to retrieve this information, the tag causes a conflict. The time taken to resolve this conflict causes negative priming.<ref name=neg/> Although both models are still valid, recent scientific research has led scientists to lean away from the distractor inhibitor model.<ref name=neg />

===Perceptual and conceptual priming===
The difference between perceptual and conceptual types of priming is whether items with a similar form or items with a similar meaning are primed, respectively.

Perceptual priming is based on the form of the stimulus and is enhanced by the match between the early and later stimuli. Perceptual priming is sensitive to the modality and exact format of the stimulus. An example of perceptual priming is the identification of an incomplete word in a [[Indirect tests of memory#Word Stem Completion .28WSC.29 Task|word-stem completion test]]. The presentation of the visual prime does not have to be perfectly consistent with later testing presentations in order to work. Studies have shown that, for example, the absolute size of the stimuli can vary and still provide significant evidence of priming.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Biederman I, Cooper EE | title=Size Invariance in Visual Object Priming | year=1992| journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance| volume=18 | issue=1| doi = 10.1037/0096-1523.18.1.121 | pages=121–133}}</ref>

Conceptual priming is based on the meaning of a stimulus and is enhanced by semantic tasks. For example, the word ''table'' will show conceptual priming effects on the word ''chair'', because the words belong to the same category.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Vaidya CJ, Gabrieli JD, Monti LA, Tinklenberg JR, Yesavage JA | title = Dissociation between two forms of conceptual priming in Alzheimer's disease | journal = Neuropsychology | volume = 13 | issue = 4 | pages = 516–24 | date = October 1999 | pmid = 10527059 | doi = 10.1037/0894-4105.13.4.516 | url = http://svalz.stanford.edu/docs/paper14.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20110719200220/http://svalz.stanford.edu/docs/paper14.pdf | archive-date = July 19, 2011 }}</ref>

===Repetition priming===
{{Main|Repetition priming}}
Repetition priming, also called direct priming, is a form of positive priming. When a stimulus is experienced, it is also primed. This means that later experiences of the stimulus will be processed more quickly by the brain.<ref name="forster">{{cite journal | vauthors = Forster KI, Davis C | title=Repetition Priming and Frequency Attenuation | year=1984| journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition| volume=10 | issue=4| pages=680–698 | doi=10.1037/0278-7393.10.4.680 }}</ref> This effect has been found on words in the [[Indirect tests of memory#Lexical Decision Task .28LDT.29|lexical decision task]]. There are multiple theories and models that reason why repetition priming might exist. For example, facilitation suggests that when a stimulus overlaps with existing or previously seen representation than information will travel faster.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=James |first1=Thomas W. |last2=Gauthier |first2=Isabel |date=January 2006 |title=Repetition-induced changes in BOLD response reflect accumulation of neural activity |journal=Human Brain Mapping |language=en |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=37–46 |doi=10.1002/hbm.20165 |issn=1065-9471 |pmc=6871272 |pmid=15954142}}</ref>

===Semantic priming===
[[File:Priming Web Diagram.svg|thumb|300px|This image shows a priming web built from different types of priming. The lines in this web indicate associations that an individual might have. If two words are more closely linked in the web, then they are more likely to be more quickly recognized when primed with a nearby word. The dotted lines indicate morpheme primes, or primes from words that sound similar to each other, while the straight lines indicate semantic primes or words that have meanings or associations that relate to each other.]]

In semantic priming, the prime and the target are from the same semantic category and share features.<ref>Ludovic Ferrand and Boris New: Semantic and associative priming in the mental lexicon, found on: boris.new.googlepages.com/Semantic-final-2003.pdf</ref> For example, the word ''dog'' is a [[semantic primes|semantic prime]] for ''wolf'', because the two are similar animals. Semantic priming is theorized to work because of [[spreading activation]] within associative networks.<ref name=reis /> When a person thinks of one item in a category, similar items are stimulated by the brain. Even if they are not words, [[morpheme]]s can prime for complete words that include them.<ref>{{cite journal | title=Morphology and Meaning in the English Mental Lexicon | vauthors = Marslen-Wilson W, Tyler LK, Waksler R, Older L | year=1994 | journal=Psychological Review | volume=101 | issue=1 | doi = 10.1037/0033-295x.101.1.3 | pages=3–33| citeseerx=10.1.1.294.7035 }}</ref> An example of this would be that the morpheme 'psych' can prime for the word 'psychology'.

In support with further detail, when an individual processes a word sometimes that word can be affected when the prior word is linked semantically. Previous studies have been conducted, focusing on priming effects having a rapid rise time and a hasty decay time. For example, an experiment by Donald Foss researched the decay time of semantic facilitation in lists and sentences. Three experiments were conducted and it was found that semantic relationships within words differs when words occur in sentences rather than lists. Thus, supporting the ongoing discourse model.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=A discourse on semantic priming|last=Foss|first=Donald|date=April 23, 1982|doi=10.1016/0010-0285(82)90020-2|pmid=7140212|volume=14|issue=4|journal=Cognitive Psychology|pages=590–607|s2cid=42931448}}</ref> However, the underlying cognitive mechanisms of semantic representation has been established over a few decades, the research has suffered from small sample sizes and lack of linguistic and cultural diversity. Nevertheless, recently it has been shown that the semantic priming is a robust and generalizable effect across languages and cultures.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Buchanan |first=Erin M. |last2=Cuccolo |first2=Kelly |last3=Heyman |first3=Tom |last4=van Berkel |first4=Niels |last5=Coles |first5=Nicholas A. |last6=Iyer |first6=Aishwarya |last7=Peters |first7=Kim |last8=van 't Veer |first8=A. E. |last9=Montefinese |first9=Maria |last10=Maxwell |first10=Nicholas P. |last11=Taylor |first11=Jack E. |last12=Valentine |first12=Kathrene D. |last13=Arriaga |first13=Patrícia |last14=Barzykowski |first14=Krystian |last15=Boucher |first15=Leanne |date=2025-09-24 |title=Measuring the semantic priming effect across many languages |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41562-025-02254-x |journal=Nature Human Behaviour |language=en |pages=1–20 |doi=10.1038/s41562-025-02254-x |issn=2397-3374|url-access=subscription |hdl=1871.1/1c6316a3-9b68-4d0e-a1e8-1cb2298b3279 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

===Associative priming===
In associative priming, the target is a word that has a high probability of appearing with the prime, and is "associated" with it but not necessarily related in semantic features. The word ''dog'' is an associative prime for ''cat'', since the words are closely associated and frequently appear together (in phrases like "raining cats and dogs").<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Matsukawa J, Snodgrass JG, Doniger GM | title = Conceptual versus perceptual priming in incomplete picture identification | journal = Journal of Psycholinguistic Research | volume = 34 | issue = 6 | pages = 515–40 | date = November 2005 | pmid = 16341912 | doi = 10.1007/s10936-005-9162-5 | url = https://kanazawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/?action=repository_action_common_download&item_id=28&item_no=1&attribute_id=26&file_no=1 | hdl = 2297/1842 | s2cid = 18396648 | hdl-access = free }}</ref> A similar effect is known as context priming. Context priming works by using a context to speed up processing for stimuli that are likely to occur in that context. A useful application of this effect is reading written text.<ref name=stano>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stanovich KE, West RF | title = On priming by a sentence context | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology: General | volume = 112 | issue = 1 | pages = 1–36 | date = March 1983 | pmid = 6221061 | doi = 10.1037/0096-3445.112.1.1 }}</ref> The grammar and vocabulary of the sentence provide contextual clues for words that will occur later in the sentence. These later words are processed more quickly than if they had been read alone, and the effect is greater for more difficult or uncommon words.<ref name=stano />

===Response priming===
{{Main|Response priming}}
In the psychology of visual perception and motor control, the term response priming denotes a special form of visuomotor priming effect. The distinctive feature of response priming is that prime and target are presented in quick succession (typically, less than 100 milliseconds apart) and are coupled to identical or alternative motor responses.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Klotz W, Wolff P | title = The effect of a masked stimulus on the response to the masking stimulus | journal = Psychological Research | volume = 58 | issue = 2 | pages = 92–101 | year = 1995 | pmid = 7480512 | doi = 10.1007/bf00571098 | s2cid = 25422424 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Klotz W, Neumann O | year = 1999 | title = Motor activation without conscious discrimination in metacontrast masking | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance | volume = 25 | issue = 4| pages = 976–992 | doi=10.1037/0096-1523.25.4.976}}</ref> When a speeded motor response is performed to classify the target stimulus, a prime immediately preceding the target can thus induce response conflicts when assigned to a different response as the target. These response conflicts have observable effects on motor behavior, leading to priming effects, e.g., in response times and error rates. A special property of response priming is its independence from visual awareness of the prime: For example, response priming effects can increase under conditions where visual awareness of the prime is decreasing.<ref>Vorberg, D., Mattler, U., Heinecke, A., Schmidt, T., & Schwarzbach, J. ''Different time courses for visual perception and action priming.'' In: ''Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA'', 100, 2003, p. 6275-6280.</ref><ref>Schmidt, T., & Vorberg, D. ''Criteria for unconscious cognition: Three types of dissociation.'' In: ''Perception & Psychophysics'', 68, 2006, p. 489-504.</ref>

===Masked priming===
The masked priming paradigm has been widely used in the last two decades in order to investigate both [[orthography|orthographic]] and [[phonological]] activations during visual [[word recognition]].
The term "masked" refers to the fact that the prime word or [[pseudoword]] is masked by symbols such as ###### that can be presented in a forward manner (before the prime) or a backward manner (after the prime). These masks enable to diminish the visibility of the prime. The prime is usually presented less than 80 ms (but typically between 40-60 ms) in this paradigm. In all, the short SOA (Stimuli Onset Asynchrony, i.e. the time delay between the onset of the mask and the prime) associated with the masking make the masked priming paradigm a good tool to investigate automatic and irrespective activations during visual word recognition.<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1037/0278-7393.10.4.680 |title=Repetition priming and frequency attenuation in lexical decision | vauthors = Forster KI, Davis C | year=1984 | journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition | pages=680–698 | volume=10 | issue=4}}</ref> Forster has argued that masked priming is a purer form of priming, as any conscious appreciation of the relationship between the prime and the target is effectively eliminated, and thus removes the subject's ability to use the prime strategically to make decisions. Results from numerous experiments show that certain forms of priming occur that are very difficult to occur with visible primes. One such example is '''form-priming''', where the prime is similar to, but not identical to the target (e.g., the words ''nature'' and ''mature'').<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/0749-596X(91)90008-8 |title=The density constraint on form-priming in the naming task: Interference effects from a masked prime | vauthors = Forster KI, Davis C | year=1984 | journal=Journal of Memory and Language | pages=1–25 | volume=30 | issue=1}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1080/14640748708401785 | title=Masked priming with graphemically related forms: Repetition or partial activation? | vauthors = Forster KI, Davis C, Schoknecht C, Carter R | year=1987 | journal=The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology A | pages=211–251 | volume=39A | issue=2| s2cid=144992122 }}</ref> Form priming is known to be affected by several psycholinguistic properties such as prime-target frequency and overlap. If a prime is higher frequency than the target, lexical competition occurs, whereas if the target has a higher frequency than the prime, then the prime pre-activates the target<ref>{{Cite journal|title= Priming word recognition with orthographic neighbors: Effects of relative prime-target frequency|url=https://doi.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037/0096-1523.16.1.65|access-date=2021-09-30|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance| year=1990 | volume=16 | issue=1 | pages=65–76 |doi=10.1037/0096-1523.16.1.65| last1=Segui | first1=Juan | last2=Grainger | first2=Jonathan | pmid=2137524 | s2cid=27807746 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> and if the prime and target differ by one letter and one phoneme, the prime competes with the target, leading to lexical competition.<ref>{{Cite journal|title= Masked inhibitory priming in English: Evidence for lexical inhibition|url=https://doi.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037/0096-1523.32.3.668|access-date=2021-09-30|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance| year=2006 |doi=10.1037/0096-1523.32.3.668| pmid=16822131 | last1=Davis | first1=C. J. | last2=Lupker | first2=S. J. | volume=32 | issue=3 | pages=668–687 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> Not only is it affected by the prime and target, but also by individual differences such that people with well-established lexical representations are more likely to show lexical competition than people with less-established lexical representation.<ref>{{Cite journal|title= Lexical precision in skilled readers: Individual differences in masked neighbor priming|url=https://doi.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037/a0018366|access-date=2021-09-30|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General| year=2010 |doi=10.1037/a0018366| pmid=20438253 | last1=Andrews | first1=S. | last2=Hersch | first2=J. | volume=139 | issue=2 | pages=299–318 | url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|title= Not all skilled readers have cracked the code: Individual differences in masked form priming|url=https://doi.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037/a0024953|access-date=2021-09-30|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition| year=2012 |doi=10.1037/a0024953| pmid=21875252 | last1=Andrews | first1=S. | last2=Lo | first2=S. | volume=38 | issue=1 | pages=152–163 | url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Perfetti|first=Charles|date=2007-09-26|title=Reading Ability: Lexical Quality to Comprehension|journal=Scientific Studies of Reading|volume=11|issue=4|pages=357–383|doi=10.1080/10888430701530730|s2cid=62203167 |issn=1088-8438}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Adelman|first1=James S.|last2=Johnson|first2=Rebecca L.|last3=McCormick|first3=Samantha F.|last4=McKague|first4=Meredith|last5=Kinoshita|first5=Sachiko|last6=Bowers|first6=Jeffrey S.|last7=Perry|first7=Jason R.|last8=Lupker|first8=Stephen J.|last9=Forster|first9=Kenneth I.|last10=Cortese|first10=Michael J.|last11=Scaltritti|first11=Michele|date=2014-12-01|title=A behavioral database for masked form priming|journal=Behavior Research Methods|language=en|volume=46|issue=4|pages=1052–1067|doi=10.3758/s13428-013-0442-y|pmid=24488815 |s2cid=935533 |issn=1554-3528|hdl=10026.1/10800|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Elsherif|first1=M.M|last2=Wheeldon|first2=L.R.|last3=Frisson|first3=S.|date=2021-09-01|title=Phonological precision for word recognition in skilled readers|journal=Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology|volume=75 |issue=6 |language=en|pages=1021–1040|doi=10.1177/17470218211046350|pmid=34467802 |pmc=9016675 |issn=1747-0218}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Elsherif |first1=M. M. |last2=Frisson |first2=S. |last3=Wheeldon |first3=L. R. |date=2022-08-25 |title=Orthographic precision for word naming in skilled readers |journal=Language, Cognition and Neuroscience |volume=38 |issue=2 |pages=197–216 |doi=10.1080/23273798.2022.2108091 |issn=2327-3798|doi-access=free |hdl=11250/3068156 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

===Kindness priming===
{{Main|Kindness priming (psychology)}}
Kindness priming is a specific form of priming that occurs when a subject experiences an act of kindness and subsequently experiences a lower threshold of activation when subsequently encountering positive stimuli. A unique feature of kindness priming is that it causes a temporarily increased resistance to negative stimuli in addition to the increased activation of positive associative networks.<ref name="carlson">Carlson, Charlin, Norman, http://psycnet.apa.org/journals/psp/55/2/211/, Positive mood and helping behavior: A test of six hypotheses, 1989</ref> This form of priming is closely related to [[affect priming]].

=== Affective priming ===
Affective or affect priming entails the evaluation of people, ideas, objects, goods, etc., not only based on the physical features of those things, but also on affective context. Most research and concepts about affective priming derive from the affective priming paradigm where people are asked to evaluate or respond to a stimuli following positive, neutral, or negative primes. Some research suggests that valence (positive vs. negative) has a stronger effect than arousal (low vs. high) on lexical-decision tasks.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Yao|first1=Zhao|last2=Zhu|first2=Xiangru|last3=Luo|first3=Wenbo|date=2019-10-01|title=Valence makes a stronger contribution than arousal to affective priming|journal=PeerJ|language=en|volume=7|article-number=e7777|doi=10.7717/peerj.7777|pmid=31592186|pmc=6777477|issn=2167-8359|doi-access=free}}</ref> Affective priming might also be more diffuse and stronger when the prime barely enters conscious awareness.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Rotteveel|first1=M.|last2=de Groot|first2=P.|last3=Geutskens|first3=A.|last4=Phaf|first4=R. H.|date=December 2001|title=Stronger suboptimal than optimal affective priming?|journal=Emotion |volume=1|issue=4|pages=348–364|doi=10.1037/1528-3542.1.4.348|issn=1528-3542|pmid=12901397}}</ref> Evaluation of emotions can be primed by other emotions as well. Thus, neutral pictures, when presented after unpleasant pictures, are perceived as more pleasant than when presented after pleasant pictures.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kosonogov|first=Vladimir|date=2020-12-30|title=The effects of the order of picture presentation on the subjective emotional evaluation of pictures|url=https://revista.appsicologia.org/index.php/rpsicologia/article/view/1608|journal=Psicologia|volume=34|issue=2|pages=171–178|doi=10.17575/psicologia.v34i2.1608|issn=2183-2471|doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Cultural priming ===
{{multiple image
| total_width       = 250
| perrow            = 2
| image1            = US Capitol and Grant Memorial.JPG
| image2            = Beijing-forbidden4.jpg
| image3            = Sarpa salpa and Chromis chromis (edited).jpg
| footer            = Study participants primed to think of the United States were more likely to describe a picture of a lone fish as being independent from its group; those primed to think of China more likely to describe it as being chased or rejected by the group.
}}
Cultural priming is a technique employed in the field of [[cross-cultural psychology]] and [[social psychology]] to understand how people interpret events and other concepts, like [[cultural frame switching]] and [[self-concept]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book | first1 = Shinobu | last1 = Kitayama | first2 = Dov | last2 = Cohen | name-list-style = vanc |title=Handbook of cultural psychology|date=2007|publisher=Guilford Press | isbn=978-1-59385-732-5|location=New York|oclc=560675525}}</ref>{{Rp|270}}<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Liu Z, Cheng M, Peng K, Zhang D | title = Self-construal priming selectively modulates the scope of visual attention | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 6 | page = 1508 | date = 2015-09-30 | pmid = 26483747 | pmc = 4588108 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2015.01508 | doi-access = free }}</ref> For example, Hong and colleagues showed participants a different set of culture related images, like the U.S. Capitol building or Chinese temples. Then participants watched a clip of a fish swimming ahead of a group of fish.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hong YY, Chiu CY, Kung TM | title = Bringing culture out in front: Effects of cultural meaning system activation on social cognition. | journal = Progress in Asian Social Psychology | date = 1997 | volume = 1 | pages = 135–46 }}</ref> After Chinese cultural priming, participants in [[Hong Kong]] were more likely to see the fish as acting in response to the group, such as agreeing with the statement, "the one fish is being chased/rejected by the other fish."<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Wang |first1=Yi-meng |last2=Wang |first2=Feng-yan |last3=Talhelm |first3=Thomas |last4=Chen |first4=Yi-qun |date=2025 |title=New evidence finds young people in Mainland China are now bicultural |journal=British Journal of Psychology |language=en |volume=116 |issue=2 |pages=460–483 |doi=10.1111/bjop.12767 |pmid=39804441 |pmc=11984341 |issn=2044-8295}}</ref> In contrast, after seeing symbols of U.S. culture, participants were more likely to see the fish as acting based on internal causes, such as agreeing with the statement, "the one fish is independent from the other fish."<ref name=":4" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hong YY, Morris MW, Chiu CY, Benet-Martínez V | title = Multicultural minds. A dynamic constructivist approach to culture and cognition | journal = The American Psychologist | volume = 55 | issue = 7 | pages = 709–20 | date = July 2000 | pmid = 10916861 | doi = 10.1037/0003-066x.55.7.709 }}</ref> In short, when people from [[Bicultural identity|bi-cultural]] societies are primed with symbols from different cultures, they are more likely to give explanations (attributions) that are more common in that culture.<ref name=":4" />

=== Anti-priming ===
Anti-priming is a measurable impairment in processing information owing to recent processing of other information when the representations of information overlap and compete. Strengthening one representation after its usage causes priming for that item but also anti-priming for some other, non-repeated items.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Marsolek|first=Chad J.|date=May 2008|title=What antipriming reveals about priming|journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences|volume=12|issue=5|pages=176–181|doi=10.1016/j.tics.2008.02.005|issn=1364-6613|pmid=18403251|s2cid=44554182}}</ref> For example, in one study, identification accuracy of old Chinese characters was significantly higher than baseline measurements (i.e., the priming effect), while identification accuracy of novel characters was significantly lower than baseline measurements (i.e., the anti-priming effect).<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Zhang|first1=Feng|last2=Fairchild|first2=Amanda J.|last3=Li|first3=Xiaoming|date=2017|title=Visual Antipriming Effect: Evidence from Chinese Character Identification|journal=Frontiers in Psychology|volume=8|page=1791|doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2017.01791|issn=1664-1078|pmc=5651016|pmid=29089908|doi-access=free}}</ref> Anti-priming is said to be the natural antithesis of repetition priming, and it manifests when two objects share component features, thereby having overlapping representations.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Humphries|first1=Ailsa|last2=Chen|first2=Zhe|last3=Wiltshire|first3=Jonathan|date=March 2020|title=Repetition priming with no antipriming in picture identification|journal=Vision Research|volume=168|pages=9–17|doi=10.1016/j.visres.2019.09.011|issn=1878-5646|pmid=32044587|s2cid=211054740|doi-access=free}}</ref> However, one study failed to find anti-priming effects in a picture-naming task even though repetition priming effects were observed. Researchers argue that anti-priming effects may not be observed in a small time-frame.<ref name=":2" />

==Replicability controversy==
{{further|Replication crisis}}
The replicability and interpretation of priming findings has become controversial.<ref name="Bower2012">B. Bower. (2012). The hot and cold of priming: Psychologists are divided on whether unnoticed cues can influence behavior. ''Science News'', '''181''', [http://www.sciencenews.org/view/feature/id/340408/description/The_Hot_and_Cold_of_Priming The Hot and Cold of Priming] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130928160208/http://www.sciencenews.org/view/feature/id/340408/description/The_Hot_and_Cold_of_Priming |date=September 28, 2013 }}</ref> Studies in 2012 failed to replicate findings, including age priming,<ref name="Doyen2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Doyen S, Klein O, Pichon CL, Cleeremans A | title = Behavioral priming: it's all in the mind, but whose mind? | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 1 | article-number = e29081 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22279526 | pmc = 3261136 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0029081 | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...729081D | doi-access = free }}</ref> with additional reports of failure to replicate this and other findings such as social-distance also reported.<ref name="Pashler2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Pashler H, Coburn N, Harris CR | title = Priming of social distance? Failure to replicate effects on social and food judgments | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 8 | article-number = e42510 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22952597 | pmc = 3430642 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0042510 | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...742510P | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name="PsychFileDrawer2013">PsychFileDrawer, (2013). [http://psychfiledrawer.org/topics/view.php?t=replicability-of-social-and-goal-priming-findings-3-849 Replicability of Social and goal priming findings].</ref>  However, some replications have been successful.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Giolla |first1=Erik Mac |last2=Karlsson |first2=Simon |last3=Neequaye |first3=David A. |last4=Bergquist |first4=Magnus |date=2024-11-12 |title=Evaluating the Replicability of Social Priming Studies |url=https://open.lnu.se/index.php/metapsychology/article/view/3308 |journal=Meta-Psychology |language=en |volume=8 |doi=10.15626/MP.2022.3308 |issn=2003-2714|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=":4" />

Priming is often considered to play a part in the success of [[sensory branding]] of products and connected to ideas like crossmodal correspondencies and sensation transference. Known effects are e.g. consumers perceiving lemonade suddenly as sweeter when the logo of the drink is more saturated towards yellow,<ref name="Sensory Marketing Aspects">{{cite journal | vauthors = Rajain P | title = Sensory Marketing Aspects: Priming, Expectations, Crossmodal Correspondences & More. | journal = Vikalpa | date = September 2016 | volume = 41 | issue = 3 | pages = 264–6 | doi = 10.1177/0256090916652045 | doi-access = free }}</ref> although this result has not yet been replicated by an independent study.

Although semantic, associative, and form priming are well established,<ref name=M2014>{{cite journal | vauthors = Meyer DE | title = Semantic priming well established | journal = Science | volume = 345 | issue = 6196 | page = 523 | date = August 2014 | pmid = 25082691 | doi = 10.1126/science.345.6196.523-b | bibcode = 2014Sci...345..523M }}</ref> some longer-term priming effects were not replicated in further studies, casting doubt on their effectiveness or even existence.<ref name=BadCopy>{{cite news|last=Yong|first=Ed |name-list-style = vanc |title=Replication studies: Bad copy|url=http://www.nature.com/news/replication-studies-bad-copy-1.10634|access-date=October 12, 2012|newspaper=Nature}}</ref> [[Nobel Prize|Nobel laureate]] and psychologist [[Daniel Kahneman]] has called on priming researchers to check the robustness of their findings in an open letter to the community, claiming that priming has become a "poster child for doubts about the integrity of psychological research."<ref>{{cite web|last=Kahneman|first=Daniel | name-list-style = vanc |title=A proposal to deal with questions about priming effects|url=http://www.nature.com/polopoly_fs/7.6716.1349271308!/suppinfoFile/Kahneman%20Letter.pdf|publisher=Nature|access-date=October 12, 2012}}</ref> In 2022, Kahneman described behavioral priming research as "effectively dead."<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adversarial Collaboration: An EDGE Lecture by Daniel Kahneman {{!}} Edge.org |url=https://www.edge.org/adversarial-collaboration-daniel-kahneman |access-date=2022-02-27 |website=www.edge.org}}</ref> Other critics have asserted that priming studies suffer from major [[publication bias]],<ref name=":3">{{cite news|last=Bower|first=Bruce | name-list-style = vanc |title=The Hot and Cold of Priming |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120313012842/http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/notrocketscience/2012/03/10/failed-replication-bargh-psychology-study-doyen/|archive-date=13 March 2012|url= http://www.sciencenews.org/view/feature/id/340408/title/The_Hot_and_Cold_of_Priming |access-date=October 12, 2012|newspaper=Science News}}</ref> [[experimenter effect]]<ref name=Doyen2012 /> and that criticism of the field is not dealt with constructively.<ref>{{cite web|last=Yong|first=Ed|title=A failed replication draws a scathing personal attack from a psychology professor|url=http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/notrocketscience/2012/03/10/failed-replication-bargh-psychology-study-doyen/|publisher=Discover Magazine|access-date=October 12, 2012|date=March 10, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120313012842/http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/notrocketscience/2012/03/10/failed-replication-bargh-psychology-study-doyen/ |archive-date=March 13, 2012 }}</ref>

==Measuring the effects of priming==
Priming effects can be found with many of the [[Indirect tests of memory|tests of implicit memory]]. Tests such as the [[Indirect tests of memory#Word stem completion (WSC) task|word-stem completion task]], and the [[Indirect tests of memory#Word fragment completion (WFC)|word fragment completion task]] measure [[#Perceptual_and_conceptual_priming|perceptual priming]]. In the word-stem completion task, participants are given a list of study words, and then asked to complete word "stems" consisting of 3 letters with the first word that comes to mind. A priming effect is observed when participants complete stems with words on the study list more often than with novel words. The word fragment completion task is similar, but instead of being given the stem of a word, participants are given a word with some letters missing. The [[Indirect tests of memory#Lexical Decision Task .28LDT.29|lexical decision task]] can be used to demonstrate [[#Types Of Priming|conceptual priming]].<ref name=MS71 /><ref name="SM73">{{cite book|title=Attention and performance IV|year=1973| vauthors = Schvaneveldt RW, Meyer DE |pages=395–409|chapter=Retrieval and comparison processes in semantic memory|place=New York|publisher=Academic Press|editor-last=Kornblum|editor-first=S.}}</ref> In this task, participants are asked to determine if a given string is a word or a nonword. Priming is demonstrated when participants are quicker to respond to words that have been primed with semantically-related words, e.g., faster to confirm "nurse" as a word when it is preceded by "doctor" than when it is preceded by "butter". Other evidence has been found through brain imaging and studies from brain injured patients. Another example of priming in healthcare research was studying if safety behaviors of nurses could be primed by structuring [[Change-of-shift report|change of shift report]].<ref name=":0">{{cite journal | vauthors = Groves PS, Bunch JL, Cram E, Farag A, Manges K, Perkhounkova Y, Scott-Cawiezell J | title = Priming Patient Safety Through Nursing Handoff Communication: A Simulation Pilot Study | journal = Western Journal of Nursing Research | volume = 39 | issue = 11 | pages = 1394–1411 | date = November 2017 | pmid = 28322631 | doi = 10.1177/0193945916673358 | s2cid = 32696412 }}</ref> A pilot [[simulation]] study found that there is early evidence to show that safety behaviors can be primed by including safety language into report.<ref name=":0" />

==Effects of brain injuries==
===Amnesia===
Patients with [[amnesia]] are described as those who have suffered damage to their [[Temporal lobe|medial temporal lobe]], resulting in the impairment of explicit recollection of everyday facts and events. Priming studies on amnesic patients have varying results, depending on both the type of priming test done, as well as the phrasing of the instructions.

Amnesic patients do as well on perceptual priming tasks as healthy patients,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cermak LS, Talbot N, Chandler K, Wolbarst LR | title = The perceptual priming phenomenon in amnesia | journal = Neuropsychologia | volume = 23 | issue = 5 | pages = 615–22 | year = 1985 | pmid = 4058707 | doi = 10.1016/0028-3932(85)90063-6 | s2cid = 30545942 }}</ref> however they show some difficulties completing [[#Types Of Priming|''conceptual priming'']] tasks, depending on the specific test. For example, they perform normally on category instance production tasks, but show impaired priming on any task that involves answering general knowledge questions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Shimamura AP, Squire LR | title=Paired-associate learning and priming effects in amnesia: a neuropsychological approach | year=1984 | journal=J. Exp. Psychol. | volume=113 | issue=4| pages=556–570| doi=10.1037/0096-3445.113.4.556| pmid=6240522 | citeseerx=10.1.1.554.414}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Blaxton TA | title = Dissociations among memory measures in memory-impaired subjects: evidence for a processing account of memory | journal = Memory & Cognition | volume = 20 | issue = 5 | pages = 549–62 | date = September 1992 | pmid = 1453972 | doi = 10.3758/BF03199587 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Phrasing of the instructions associated with the test has had a dramatic impact on an amnesic's ability to complete the task successfully. When performing a [[Indirect tests of memory#Word Stem Completion .28WSC.29 Task|word-stem completion]] test, patients were able to successfully complete the task when asked to complete the stem using the first word that came to mind, but when explicitly asked to recall a word to complete the stem that was on the study list, patients performed at below-average levels.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Graf P, Squire LR, Mandler G | title = The information that amnesic patients do not forget | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition | volume = 10 | issue = 1 | pages = 164–78 | date = January 1984 | pmid = 6242734 | doi = 10.1037/0278-7393.10.1.164 }}</ref>

Overall, studies from amnesic patients indicate that priming is controlled by a brain system separate from the medial temporal system that supports [[explicit memory]].

===Aphasia===
Perhaps the first use of semantic priming in neurological patients was with stroke patients with [[aphasia]]. In one study, patients with Wernicke's aphasia who were unable to make semantic judgments showed evidence of semantic priming, while patients with [[Broca's Aphasia|Broca's aphasia]] who were able to make semantic judgments showed less consistent priming than those with [[Wernicke's aphasia]] or normal controls. This dissociation was extended to other linguistic categories such phonology and syntactic processing by Blumstein, Milberg and their colleagues.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=1866|title=Aphasia|journal=The British Medical Journal|volume=2|issue=296|pages=258–261|jstor=25205881|issn=0007-1447}}</ref>

===Dementia===
Patients with [[Alzheimer's disease]] (AD), the most common form of dementia, have been studied extensively as far as priming goes. Results are conflicting in some cases, but overall, patients with AD show decreased priming effects on [[Indirect tests of memory#Word Stem Completion .28WSC.29 Task|word-stem completion]] and [[Free association (psychology)|free association]] tasks, while retaining normal performance on [[Indirect tests of memory#Lexical Decision Task .28LDT.29|lexical decision tasks]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Carlesimo GA, Oscar-Berman M | title = Memory deficits in Alzheimer's patients: a comprehensive review | journal = Neuropsychology Review | volume = 3 | issue = 2 | pages = 119–69 | date = June 1992 | pmid = 1300219 | doi = 10.1007/BF01108841 | s2cid = 19548915 }}</ref> These results suggest that AD patients are impaired in any sort of priming task that requires semantic processing of the stimuli, while priming tasks that require visuoperceptual interpretation of stimuli are unaffected by Alzheimers.

===Focal cortical lesions===
Patient J.P., who suffered a stroke in the left medial/temporal gyrus, resulting in [[auditory verbal agnosia]] - the inability to comprehend spoken words, but maintaining the ability to read and write, and with no effects to hearing ability. J.P. showed normal [[#Types Of Priming|perceptual priming]], but his conceptual priming ability for spoken words was, expectedly, impaired.<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1037/0894-4105.7.2.107 | vauthors = Schacter DL, McGlynn SM, Milberg WP, Church BA | year=1993 | title=Spared priming despite impaired comprehension: implicit memory in a case of word meaning deafness | journal=Neuropsychology | volume=7 | pages=107–118 | issue=2| s2cid = 3813751 }}</ref> Another patient, N.G., who suffered from prosopanomia (the inability to retrieve proper names) following damage to his left temporal lobe, was unable to spontaneously provide names of persons or cities, but was able to successfully complete a word-fragment completion exercise following priming with these names. This demonstrated intact perceptual priming abilities.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Geva A, Moscovitch M, Leach L | title = Perceptual priming of proper names in young and older normal adults and a patient with prosopanomia | journal = Neuropsychology | volume = 11 | issue = 2 | pages = 232–42 | date = April 1997 | pmid = 9110330 | doi = 10.1037/0894-4105.11.2.232 }}</ref>

==Cognitive neuroscience==
===Perceptual priming===
[[File:Brodmann areas 17 18 19.png|thumb|The [[extrastriate cortex]] (shown in orange and red) is believed to be involved in perceptual priming.]]
Priming while improving performance decreases neural processing in the [[cerebral cortex]] of sensory stimuli with stimulus repetition. This has been found in single-cell recordings<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Li L, Miller EK, Desimone R | title = The representation of stimulus familiarity in anterior inferior temporal cortex | journal = Journal of Neurophysiology | volume = 69 | issue = 6 | pages = 1918–29 | date = June 1993 | pmid = 8350131 | doi = 10.1152/jn.1993.69.6.1918 | s2cid = 6482631 }}</ref> and in [[electroencephalography]] (EEG) upon [[gamma wave]]s,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gruber T, Müller MM | title = Effects of picture repetition on induced gamma band responses, evoked potentials, and phase synchrony in the human EEG | journal = Brain Research. Cognitive Brain Research | volume = 13 | issue = 3 | pages = 377–92 | date = May 2002 | pmid = 11919002 | doi = 10.1016/S0926-6410(01)00130-6 }}</ref> with [[Positron Emission Tomography|PET]]<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Squire LR, Ojemann JG, Miezin FM, Petersen SE, Videen TO, Raichle ME | title = Activation of the hippocampus in normal humans: a functional anatomical study of memory | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 89 | issue = 5 | pages = 1837–41 | date = March 1992 | pmid = 1542680 | pmc = 48548 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.89.5.1837 | bibcode = 1992PNAS...89.1837S | doi-access = free }}</ref> and [[functional MRI]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wig GS, Grafton ST, Demos KE, Kelley WM | title = Reductions in neural activity underlie behavioral components of repetition priming | journal = Nature Neuroscience | volume = 8 | issue = 9 | pages = 1228–33 | date = September 2005 | pmid = 16056222 | doi = 10.1038/nn1515 | s2cid = 470957 }}</ref> This reduction is due to representational sharpening in the early sensory areas which reduces the number of neurons representing the stimulus. This leads to a more selective activation of neurons representing objects in higher cognitive areas.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Moldakarimov S, Bazhenov M, Sejnowski TJ | title = Perceptual priming leads to reduction of gamma frequency oscillations | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 107 | issue = 12 | pages = 5640–5 | date = March 2010 | pmid = 20212165 | pmc = 2851786 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0907525107 | bibcode = 2010PNAS..107.5640M | doi-access = free }}</ref>

===Conceptual priming===
Conceptual priming has been linked to reduced blood flow in the left [[prefrontal cortex]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Demb JB, Desmond JE, Wagner AD, Vaidya CJ, Glover GH, Gabrieli JD |author6-link=John Gabrieli |author5-link=Gary H. Glover| title = Semantic encoding and retrieval in the left inferior prefrontal cortex: a functional MRI study of task difficulty and process specificity | journal = The Journal of Neuroscience | volume = 15 | issue = 9 | pages = 5870–8 | date = September 1995 | pmid = 7666172 | pmc = 6577672 | doi = 10.1523/JNEUROSCI.15-09-05870.1995 }}</ref> The left prefrontal cortex is believed to be involved in the semantic processing of words, among other tasks.<ref>{{cite journal |author2-link=Russell Poldrack | vauthors = Gabrieli JD, Poldrack RA, Desmond JE | title = The role of left prefrontal cortex in language and memory | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 95 | issue = 3 | pages = 906–13 | date = February 1998 | pmid = 9448258 | pmc = 33815 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.95.3.906 | bibcode = 1998PNAS...95..906G | doi-access = free }}</ref>

The view that perceptual priming is controlled by the extrastriate cortex while conceptual priming is controlled by the left prefrontal cortex is undoubtedly an oversimplified view of the process, and current work is focused on elucidating the brain regions involved in priming in more detail.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = [[Stanislas Dehaene|Dehaene S]], Naccache L, Le Clec'H G, Koechlin E, Mueller M, [[Ghislaine Dehaene-Lambertz|Dehaene-Lambertz G]], van de Moortele PF, Le Bihan D | title = Imaging unconscious semantic priming | journal = Nature | volume = 395 | issue = 6702 | pages = 597–600 | date = October 1998 | pmid = 9783584 | doi = 10.1038/26967 | url = http://www.casra.ch/teaching/2002/cns/doc/Dehaene_et_al_1998.pdf | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20110706222851/http://www.casra.ch/teaching/2002/cns/doc/Dehaene_et_al_1998.pdf | bibcode = 1998Natur.395..597D | s2cid = 4373890 | archive-date = July 6, 2011 }}</ref>

==In daily life==
Priming is thought to play a large part in the systems of stereotyping.<ref name=auto>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bargh JA, Chen M, Burrows L | title = Automaticity of social behavior: direct effects of trait construct and stereotype-activation on action | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 71 | issue = 2 | pages = 230–44 | date = August 1996 | pmid = 8765481 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.71.2.230 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.318.3898 | s2cid = 6654763 }}</ref> This is because attention to a response increases the frequency of that response, even if the attended response is undesired.
<ref name=auto /><ref>(see [[Ironic process theory]] for a more in-depth discussion of this phenomenon)</ref> The attention given to these response or behaviors primes them for later activation.<ref name=auto /> Another way to explain this process is [[automaticity]]. If trait descriptions, for instance "stupid" or "friendly", have been frequently or recently used, these descriptions can be automatically used to interpret someone's behavior. An individual is unaware of this, and this may lead to behavior that may not agree with their personal beliefs.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bargh JA, Williams EL | title = The Automaticity of Social Life | journal = Current Directions in Psychological Science | volume = 15 | issue = 1 | pages = 1–4 | date = February 2006 | pmid = 18568084 | pmc = 2435044 | doi = 10.1111/j.0963-7214.2006.00395.x }}</ref>

This can occur even if the subject is not conscious of the priming stimulus.<ref name=auto /> An example of this was done by [[John Bargh]] et al. in 1996. Subjects were implicitly primed with words related to the stereotype of elderly people (example: Florida, forgetful, wrinkle). While the words did not explicitly mention speed or slowness, those who were primed with these words walked more slowly upon exiting the testing booth than those who were primed with neutral stimuli.<ref name=auto /> Similar effects were found with rude and polite stimuli: those primed with rude words were more likely to interrupt an investigator than those primed with neutral words, and those primed with polite words were the least likely to interrupt.<ref name=auto /> A 2008 study showed that something as simple as holding a hot or cold beverage before an interview could result in pleasant or negative opinion of the interviewer.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Williams LE, Bargh JA | title = Experiencing physical warmth promotes interpersonal warmth | journal = Science | volume = 322 | issue = 5901 | pages = 606–7 | date = October 2008 | pmid = 18948544 | pmc = 2737341 | doi = 10.1126/science.1162548 | bibcode = 2008Sci...322..606W }}</ref>

These findings have been extended to therapeutic interventions. For example, a 2012 study suggested that presented with a depressed patient who "self-stereotypes herself as incompetent, a therapist can find ways to prime her with specific situations in which she had been competent in the past... Making memories of her competence more salient should reduce her self-stereotype of incompetence."<ref
name="Cox et al. (2012)">{{cite journal | vauthors = Cox WT, Abramson LY, Devine PG, Hollon SD | title = Stereotypes, Prejudice, and Depression: The Integrated Perspective | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 5 | pages = 427–49 | date = September 2012 | pmid = 26168502 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612455204 | s2cid = 1512121 }}</ref>

== See also ==
* [[Intertrial priming]]
* [[Implicit association test]]
* [[Priming (media)]]
* [[Framing (social sciences)]]
* [[Overton window]]
